one_line_takeaway: A diagnosis-driven, drop-in refinement of GCG (distance-regularized selection + temperature sampling + duplicate marking) that cuts jailbreak optimization cost ~8x while modestly raising attack success on aligned LLMs.
key_finding: Under a low budget (B=64), Faster-GCG matches or beats GCG run at 8x the budget. On JBB the base Faster-GCG averages 69.5% ASR vs GCG's 65.8% (a 3.7% avg gain), and the enhanced Faster-GCG++ (40-token prepended suffix) reaches 78.1% average ASR, the best among all white-box and black-box baselines, including 88.7% on Qwen3.5-4B. The distance regularization lifts ranking CCC from 0.044 (gradient-only, essentially no correlation) to 0.285 (lambda=10). Faster-GCG at B=64 (loss 0.268, ASR 66.0%, 7 min) is comparable to GCG at B=512 (loss 0.247, ASR 65.5%, 49 min), evidencing ~8x sample and ~7x time efficiency.
core_contribution: Faster-GCG is a drop-in efficiency upgrade to the Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) white-box jailbreak attack on aligned LLMs. The authors diagnose three specific sources of GCG's sample inefficiency and fix each with a simple technique: (1) Distance-regularized token selection, which adds a penalty lambda*||W_E[i]-v_t|| to the gradient score so candidate tokens far from the current token in embedding space (where the first-order Taylor approximation is unreliable) are down-ranked; (2) Temperature-controlled replacement sampling, which replaces GCG's uniform sampling over the top-K set with a rank-based temperature distribution (tau) that biases toward higher-gradient candidates while retaining exploration; and (3) Visited-suffix marking, which hashes and records already-evaluated suffixes so duplicates are never re-queried. Combined, these let Faster-GCG reach GCG-comparable loss/ASR with roughly 8x fewer model evaluations (256K down to ~32K) and ~7x less wall-clock time. This is the paper's OWN contribution; GCG, I-GCG, GCG-PS, TAO and AutoDAN are prior/baseline work.
threat_model: White-box adversary (Appendix B) with full access to target model parameters and gradients of the loss w.r.t. input tokens. The attacker may construct any user input but must preserve the chat template (system/assistant roles unmodified, no assistant-response pre-filling), and is bounded by a finite optimization/query budget. The attack appends (or, in the ++ variant, prepends) an optimized adversarial suffix x_adv to a harmful query to maximize the likelihood of a target affirmative response (e.g. 'Sure, here is...'). Notably the paper stresses that the target string is only a surrogate objective: success is judged at the behavior level (does the model actually produce the harmful content), not by verbatim reproduction of the target string.
limitations: By the authors' own statement, Faster-GCG inherits GCG's requirement of white-box access to model weights and gradients; transfer to proprietary models is possible but finding a good white-box surrogate remains difficult. Reported defense-robustness results show ASRs drop under existing defenses (though Faster-GCG still beats GCG). Reviewer-noted caveats: only the first 100 of AdvBench's 520 behaviors are used; ASR depends on LLM-judge scoring rather than human evaluation; and several baseline cells are missing (AutoDAN/GCG-PS on Qwen3.5, Faster-I-GCG on Llama3.1/Qwen3.5) because the authors could not adapt or found the baseline ineffective, so the headline 78.1% average for Faster-GCG++ is not compared against a fully-populated baseline row.

quantitative_results: Evaluations per attack reduced from ~256K (GCG: B=512, S=500) to ~32K, ~8x sample-efficiency and ~7x wall-clock improvement
JBB average ASR: Faster-GCG 69.5% vs GCG 65.8% (base config); Faster-GCG++ 78.1% (best overall)
Faster-GCG++ ASR on Qwen3.5-4B: 88.7% (loss 0.149) on JBB
Ranking agreement CCC improved from 0.044 (gradient-only) to 0.285 (distance regularization, lambda=10), over 640 samples
~5.9% (~6%) of GCG evaluated suffixes were redundant revisits
Table 2 (Llama2-7B): Faster-GCG B=64 -> loss 0.268, ASR 66.0%, 7 min; GCG B=512 -> loss 0.247, ASR 65.5%, 49 min; GCG B=64 -> 0.350, 58.2%
JBB per-model ASR, Faster-GCG (base): Llama2-7B 66.0%, Qwen3-4B 54.2%, Gemma3-4B 81.4%, Llama3.1-8B 67.1%, Qwen3.5-4B 78.7%
AdvBench average ASR: GCG 53.4%, TAO 58.5%, Faster-GCG 59.0%, Faster-GCG++ 73.4%
Ablation (JBB/Llama2-7B): full method loss 0.268/ASR 66.0% vs GCG baseline 0.350/58.2%; fixed hyperparameters lambda=10, tau=0.1
baselines_compared: Black-box attacks in Appendix G.1 (e.g. PAIR, FlipAttack, persuasion-based)
